reasoning: I will extract and present the causes of rebound (medication-overuse) headaches as described in the provided text, focusing on how frequent or inappropriate use of various headache medications leads to headaches.
output:
Causes of rebound headaches (medication-overuse headaches):
- Frequent use of headache medications. Any acute headache medicine can contribute, with risk depending on the drug.
- Simple pain relievers (aspirin, acetaminophen): may contribute to rebound headaches, especially if you exceed the recommended daily dosages.
- NSAIDs (ibuprofen, naproxen): have a relatively low risk but can contribute.
- Combination pain relievers (OTC combos containing caffeine, aspirin, and acetaminophen; e.g., Excedrin): are common culprits.
- Butalbital-containing compounds (e.g., Fiorinal): have an especially high risk; limit use to no more than four days a month.
- Migraine medications: triptans (Imitrex, Zomig, others) and certain ergots: moderate risk.
- Ergotamine-containing drugs (ergot derivatives): moderate risk; dihydroergotamine (D.H.E. 45) appears to have lower potential.
- Opiates (codeine-containing combinations, etc.): high risk.
- Daily caffeine: regular caffeine from coffee, soda, and products containing caffeine can fuel rebound headaches; check labels to avoid excess caffeine.
- Risk factors include: history of chronic headaches (migraine or tension-type) and frequent use of headache medications.
  - Increased risk if using combination analgesics, ergotamine, or triptans 10 or more days per month, or simple analgesics more than 15 days a month; especially when regular use continues for three or more months.
